title: US Weekly Gas Price Volatility
text:
Dataset

weekly_gas_prices <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-07-01/weekly_gas_prices.csv')
# code source: https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2025/2025-07-01/readme.md

glimpse(weekly_gas_prices) #gets overview of data

Rows: 22,360
Columns: 5
$ date        <date> 1990-08-20, 1990-08-20, 1990-08-27, 1990-08-27, 1990-09-0…
$ fuel        <chr> "gasoline", "gasoline", "gasoline", "gasoline", "gasoline"…
$ grade       <chr> "regular", "regular", "regular", "regular", "regular", "re…
$ formulation <chr> "all", "conventional", "all", "conventional", "all", "conv…
$ price       <dbl> 1.191, 1.191, 1.245, 1.245, 1.242, 1.242, 1.252, 1.252, 1.…


This dataset was taken from the TidyTuesday github repo from 2025-07-01. It looks at the weekly US gas prices for both regular and diesel. The date range of this data set is from Aug 1990 to June 2025. I mainly chose this data set because I wanted to explore some of the peaks of previous spikes in gas prices, especially with the volatility of gas prices so far in 2026.
From the TidyTuesday README: “The data comes from the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA), which publishes average retail gasoline and diesel prices each Monday.” The original data can be found at Gasoline and Diesel Fuel Update - U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA)

title: US Weekly Gas Price Volatility
text:
Questions
Are sudden spikes in gas prices common, or do prices rise at a relatively constant rate?
Are there differences in volatility of prices between regular and diesel?

title: US Weekly Gas Price Volatility
text:
Analysis plan

For the first question, I will only look at regular gas prices. Also, to make it consistent over time, I will look at “regular” grade and “all” formulation. I will add two new variables to calculate the change in price from the previous week and the percent change in order to visualize trends in “spikes.”

To determine what I will consider a “spike”, I will look at the top 1%, 5%, and 10%.percent weekly increases.
I will create two different visualizations. I will create a histogram to see how common “spikes” are. I will also create a time series to show when these “spikes occur”
If I discover a spike, I will research to see if there were any significant world events at the time and create captions for any siginificant stories.

For the second question, I will need the percent change as in the first question, but I am more concerned with the movement in either direction, so I will use the absolute value of the change to measure volatility. I will need to compare the “fuel” variable, selecting “gasoline” and “diesel”

For this visualization I will use a rolling standard deviation.

title: Project title
text:
Math Expressions
You can write LaTeX math expressions inside a pair of dollar signs, e.g. $\alpha+\beta$ renders \(\alpha + \beta\). You can use the display style with double dollar signs:
$$\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i$$
\[
\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i
\]
Limitations:

The source code of a LaTeX math expression must be in one line, unless it is inside a pair of double dollar signs, in which case the starting $$ must appear in the very beginning of a line, followed immediately by a non-space character, and the ending $$ must be at the end of a line, led by a non-space character;
There should not be spaces after the opening $ or before the closing $.

title: Project title
text:
Feeling adventurous?

You are welcomed to use the default styling of the slides. In fact, that’s what I expect majority of you will do. You will differentiate yourself with the content of your presentation.
But some of you might want to play around with slide styling. Some solutions for this can be found at https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/revealjs.
